title: Calculating winter feed requirements for livestock
subtitle: Working out how much hay, haylage, and concentrates to buy in before the season.
excerpt: Running out of hay in February is avoidable. A straightforward calculation, stock numbers multiplied by daily intake multiplied by the number of housed days, gives you the tonnage to buy in August when prices are lowest and quality is best. Here's how to work it out for sheep, cattle, and goats.
sourceNotes: AHDB Beef and Lamb, Feed Planning Guide. SAC Consulting Scotland, Forage Planning for Small Herds. DEFRA/APHA livestock welfare guidance.

glossaryTerms.definition: The portion of a feed that remains after all water is removed; used to compare feeds with different moisture contents on a like-for-like basis.
Partially dried and fermented grass, baled at higher moisture than hay and wrapped in plastic; lower dust than hay and often more nutritious, but requires anaerobic storage.

aliases: how much hay do I need

content.text: The calculation for winter feed has three inputs: the number of animals, the daily feed intake per animal, and the number of days they'll be housed or unable to graze. Multiply these together and add 10% as a buffer. For sheep and goats, daily
intake from forage is around 2,3% of bodyweight; a 65 kg ewe needs approximately 1.5 kg of hay or equivalent per day from forage alone.

A flock of 20 ewes averaging 65 kg, housed from November to April (150 days), each eating 1.5 kg of hay per day: 20 × 1.5 × 150 = 4,500 kg of hay (4.5 tonnes). Add 10% as a buffer for waste, wet weather, and pregnant ewes with higher needs: 4.95 tonnes. Order 5 tonnes minimum.

Ewes in the last six weeks of pregnancy and the first six weeks of lactation need 30,50% more dry matter intake than a maintenance ewe. In a lambing flock, the months of February through April are peak demand. Plan the calculation with this uplift applied to the last eight weeks of your housed period.

at 60% dry matter content contains 40% water; hay at 85% dry matter is much drier. If you're switching between them, the tonnage calculation changes. To compare: a bale of hay weighing 250 kg at 85% DM contains 212 kg of actual feed; a bale of wrapped haylage at 300 kg at 60% DM contains 180 kg of actual feed. Adjust the number of bales accordingly.

Buy hay in August when the harvest is in. Store under cover or under a tarpaulin with good airflow. Hay stored directly on the ground without pallets or dunnage absorbs ground moisture from below and deteriorates quickly. Stack bales no more than four high for safe handling.
